retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: When troubleshooting poor-quality radiographic images, the first step should be adjusting the postprocessing parameters to see if the image can be reproduced better. One should also optimize image acquisition and processing protocols to avoid repeat examinations of the patients and unnecessary radiation exposure.

retrieved_docs.title: Iridocorneal Dysgenesis -- Evaluation -- Primary Congenital Glaucoma
retrieved_docs.content: Examining under anesthesia (EUA) is an important technique for evaluating pediatric patients suspected of having the disease. The corneal diameter, intraocular pressure, direct gonioscopy, pachymetry, fundus examination, and axial length are a few important tools in the armamentarium of ophthalmologists to evaluate glaucoma. Serial retinoscopy on each visit ensures early detection of progressive myopia, which is a surrogate marker of disease progression. [65] The angle will appear normal in iris hypoplasia, unlike infantile congenital glaucoma, where high iris insertion will be visible. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Gallbladder Imaging -- Plain Films
retrieved_docs.content: There are several diagnostic tests used to determine gallbladder disease. A simple abdominal X-ray can be used to identify calcified gallstones. Because only 10% of all gallstones are calcified, this imaging study has limited usefulness. Porcelain gallbladders can also be seen in plain X-rays. This condition is somewhat uncommon and results from calcification of the gallbladder wall. In 1924, 2 American surgeons developed the oral cholecystogram or OCG. The OCG is done by administering iopanoic acid by mouth. This is an iodine-based material absorbed by the intestines and concentrated in the gallbladder. Combined with bile salts, it becomes a radio-opaque liquid within the gallbladder. This outlines any gallstones present in the gallbladder. The intervenous cholangiogram was developed in 1954. It was chiefly used to evaluate the bile ducts and to look for stones or strictures within these bile ducts. An iodine-based dye is injected intravenously. It is then concentrated in the liver and excreted into the bile ducts. The intervenous cholangiogram was a poor test to evaluate the actual gallbladder because the gallbladder can be bypassed entirely as the dye went directly from the bile ducts into the small bowel. Both the oral cholecystogram and the intravenous cholecystogram are seldom used today.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Image quality can be defined as the attribute of the image that influences the clinician's certainty to perceive the appropriate diagnostic features from the image visually. [1] [2] Quality assurance or improvement is the proactive action to enhance the quality of care and services and cost-effectively remove waste. This topic discusses the fundamental concepts of digital radiographic image quality assurance. The most common digital radiographic detectors are computed radiography (CR) and digital radiography (DR). The important components of radiographic image quality include contrast, dynamic range, spatial resolution, noise, and artifacts. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In summary, we discussed important components of radiographic image quality and various factors affecting image quality. This knowledge is useful for obtaining high-quality digital radiographs with the lowest possible radiation dose to improve the clinician's diagnostic accuracy.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Definition/Introduction -- Noise
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic noise is the random or structured variation within an image that does not correspond to X-ray attenuation variations of the object. The noise power spectrum is the best noise metric that measures the noise's spatial frequency content. [3] Quantum noise is primarily responsible for image noise, and the number of X-ray quanta used to form the image determines the quantum noise. Controlling exposure factors is the best way to reduce quantum noise.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Ray Image Quality Assurance -- Issues of Concern -- Acquisition Geometry
retrieved_docs.content: Image acquisition geometric factors affecting image quality include source-to-image receptor distance, orientation, magnification amount, and focal spot size. Changes in source-to-image receptor distance result in variations in relative magnifications of anatomic structures in the image.

retrieved_docs.title: Uveitis Glaucoma Hyphema Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Glaucoma evaluation can be performed by gonioscopy, central corneal thickness, perimetry, and optical coherence tomography of the optic nerve. All the other causes of uveitis need to be ruled out. An individualized approach should be used to investigate such cases and may include complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, serology for human immunodeficiency virus, Mantoux test, autoimmune antibody assays, chest x-ray, x-ray of the spine, and lumbosacral joint in both anterior-posterior and lateral views. [58] Optical coherence tomography of the macula can detect cystoid macular edema in UGH cases. Fundus fluorescein angiography helps to rule out the other causes of vitreous hemorrhage or iris new vessels. [59] A coagulation profile and a history of anticoagulant medications should be sought in cases with recurrent hyphema in UGH syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Trachoma -- Evaluation -- Radiography and Imaging Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic imaging: Radiographic imaging techniques, such as optical coherence tomography or ultrasonography, can be employed to assess structural abnormalities in the eye, particularly in cases of corneal opacity or scarring. Extra diagnostic testing: Conjunctival photography and mobile health technology serve as supplementary tools used in certain situations to record clinical observations, monitor and track the course of an illness, and assess treatment effectiveness. Modern techniques helpful for trachoma diagnosis include photography, [55] artificial machine learning approaches, [56] and automated deep learning methods. [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Evaluation -- Computed Tomography Scan
retrieved_docs.content: In suspected fractures, a noncontrast computed tomography (CT) scan is preferable.

retrieved_docs.content: The factors affecting image quality include:

retrieved_docs.title: Assessment of the Watery Eye -- Evaluation -- Computed tomographic DCG
retrieved_docs.content: This technique involves obtaining axial and coronal (1–2-mm thickness) computed tomographic images after using contrast media. The contrast media (iohexol, iopamidol) could be injected in the inferior or superior canaliculus or applied topically every minute for 5 minutes or 3 times at 0, 5, and 10 minutes. The main advantage is the information about the surrounding bony structures, which is relevant in assessing nasolacrimal obstructions in cases of maxillofacial trauma, congenital craniofacial deformities, previous surgery, lacrimal drainage tumors, and associated sinus disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Assessment of the Watery Eye -- Evaluation -- Digitally subtracted DCG
retrieved_docs.content: This technique leads to bone-free images of the lacrimal drainage system after injecting contrast media (iohexol, iopamidol, sinograffin) in the superior or inferior canaliculus; it  is indicated in complex lacrimal disorders. Disadvantages of this technique include the use of ionizing radiation and contrast media, lack of information about the surrounding bony structures, inability to assess the tear flow dynamics, and a limited view of the proximal lacrimal system. [60] Despite these limitations, it is considered the gold standard for evaluating obstruction of the lacrimal drainage system.

retrieved_docs.title: Pars Interarticularis Defect(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Plain radiographs can miss some lesions, especially acute injury. Other useful imaging modalities include CT, MRI, and bone scan. A bone scan is the most sensitive modality, and best detects early pars defects. This test should be utilized when there is high clinical suspicion for pars lesion, and initial imaging studies are equivocal. After plain radiographs, the imaging modality of choice is either MRI without contrast or CT scan without contrast. CT scan is the best imaging tool for determining fracture size and extent and is the most appropriate modality for follow-up assessment of healing.  CT has the downside of additional radiation exposure, which is particularly concerning in the pediatric & adolescent population. Similar to a bone scan, MRI can be useful for early detection of acute lesions by the presence of bone marrow edema on T2 weighted sequences. Additionally,  MRI has the benefit of no radiation exposure. MRI is somewhat limited, though, in its ability to adequately depict the cortical integrity of incomplete fractures. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 853
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow-up is classified as a Computed tomography imaging ‑ action and functions as a method of Unlisted computed tomography procedure (eg, diagnostic, interventional). This indicates that CT follow-up represents a specific imaging approach used when a standard listed CT procedure is not applicable, serving as a methodological option within broader diagnostic and interventional CT practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 448
retrieved_docs.content: as a child modality, indicating that tomography (including CT) is considered a specialized subcategory within the spectrum of plain radiographic techniques. This network of hierarchical, mapping, and metadata relationships integrates various CT examinations—both non‑contrast and contrast‑enhanced, single‑region and multi‑region—under unified anatomical and procedural categories, supporting consistent terminology and facilitating comprehensive patient care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2941
retrieved_docs.content: CT follow-up is identified as a method_of, meaning it serves as a method of Computed tomography imaging - action, providing a structured approach for using CT scans in follow‑up assessments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: answer annotation (“诊断或分期操作:类型:时间点:^患者:名义型:PhenX”), denoting its function as a diagnostic or staging operation within phenotypic data frameworks. Together, these hierarchical, methodological, and clinical relationships illustrate an integrated imaging network in which routine chest radiography anchors a wide array of CT protocols across multiple body regions, organized within broader radiographic and tomographic classification systems to support comprehensive patient evaluation and research data collection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1218
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast, Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast, and the two diagnostic computed tomography procedures of the thorax—one performed without contrast material and another performed without contrast material followed by contrast material and additional sections—are all specific modalities that fall under the broader category of a chest CT scan. These relationships indicate that each of these named CT techniques is a subtype (“isa”) or a more narrowly defined variant of the general “ct scan chest” procedure, reflecting differences in contrast use and imaging phases. In addition, the imaging entry labeled “Other chest x‑ray” is linked to the Arabic‑language term “التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر” (computed tomography of the chest) as a parent concept, positioning this x‑ray study within the same overarching chest imaging hierarchy. Collectively, the hierarchy demonstrates that various CT thorax protocols—whether with contrast, without contrast, or with combined phases—are organized as specialized forms of chest CT, while other chest radiographic examinations are considered related imaging modalities within the same parent imaging domain. This structured classification aids clinicians in selecting the appropriate chest imaging technique based on diagnostic needs and contrast requirements.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2366
retrieved_docs.content: Plain X-ray techniques encompass tomografie as a subordinate imaging method, indicating that tomografie is classified as a child technique within the broader category of plain X-ray imaging. This relationship highlights that tomografie derives from or is considered a specialized extension of standard plain X-ray procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 464
retrieved_docs.content: Computer assisted surgery (CAS) is categorized as a subfield of other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, and it is also placed under the domains of magnetic resonance imaging and other CT scan modalities, reflecting that CAS relies on and integrates imaging technologies from radiology, MRI, and CT to guide and enhance surgical procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 480
retrieved_docs.content: tomography imaging, and shared anatomical targets. This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding the placement of each imaging modality within the larger diagnostic radiology framework, facilitating appropriate selection and interpretation of studies for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 291
retrieved_docs.content: Tomografie is recognized as a medical diagnostic imaging modality; it is classified within the broader category of plain X‑ray techniques and is considered a specific type of medical diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are specialized forms of a ct scan chest, representing specific imaging techniques that use contrast to evaluate thoracic structures. A ct scan chest serves as the imaging modality for identifying thoracic pathology, and when a computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum is performed, the finding is obtained through the ct scan chest method. Thus, chest CT, including its contrast‑enhanced angiographic and axial tomography variants, is the primary diagnostic tool for visualizing and characterizing a mass of the mid mediastinum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 383
retrieved_docs.content: how a simple chest X‑ray can serve as the gateway to a hierarchy of imaging techniques—from plain radiography to advanced CT of multiple body regions—while also connecting to specific disease associations and broader neuro‑imaging frameworks essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1289
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are specific procedures that fall under the broader category of ct scan chest. In addition, Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections represent narrower subcategories of ct scan chest, indicating non‑contrast thoracic CT and a sequential protocol that begins without contrast and then adds contrast material with additional sections. Together, these entities illustrate the hierarchical relationship among chest CT techniques, where the general ct scan chest encompasses both contrast‑enhanced angiographic studies and diagnostic thoracic CT examinations performed with or without contrast, each selected to provide appropriate anatomical and vascular information for patient evaluation.

retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the chest revealing a miliary pulmonary infiltrate is a radiologic finding that occurs in the lungs.

retrieved_docs.content: Digital subtraction angiography of the aorta and lower limb performed with bilateral data acquisition runs of ≤ 3, 4–6, 7–9, or ≥ 10 is primarily mapped from a contrast aortogram, indicating that each of these specific angiographic protocols derives its primary imaging reference from a contrast‑enhanced aortographic study.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2420
retrieved_docs.content: Arteriography is a diagnostic imaging technique that is used to visualize arteries, providing detailed images of arterial structures for clinical assessment and patient care.
